answers:
**1. Root cause of the cross-tenant data leak.**
The responsible files are `cedarline/.env` (and `.env.example`) together with `cedarline/backend/app/db.py`. `DATABASE_URL` is set to `postgresql://cedar_owner:ownerpw@localhost:5432/cedar`, and `db.py` builds its single SQLAlchemy `engine` straight from that `DATABASE_URL`. `cedar_owner` is the role docker-compose creates via `POSTGRES_USER: cedar_owner` in `cedarline/docker-compose.yml` — the Postgres cluster's bootstrap/initdb role, which is a Postgres superuser. Superuser (and any `BYPASSRLS`) connections bypass row-level security **unconditionally and silently** — there is no error or warning, the `USING (tenant_id = current_setting('app.current_tenant')::uuid)` policies defined on `clients` and `appointments` in `db/migrations/0001_init.sql` simply never get evaluated for that connection. So even though `backend/app/deps.py`'s `tenant_connection()` dutifully does `SET app.current_tenant = :tenant_id` and `main.py`'s `GET /clients` handler relies on RLS instead of an explicit `WHERE tenant_id = ...` filter, none of that matters: because the connection is opened as `cedar_owner`, Postgres returns every row in `clients` regardless of the tenant setting. The dedicated least-privileged role `cedar_app` (created in `db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`, with only `SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE` and no bypass privilege) is exactly the role RLS would actually apply to — but the application was never configured to use it. This has been true since the very first commit (`a24f68f`, CED-1), and the later CED-7 commit added the app role and an isolation test suite that correctly uses `cedar_app`, but never updated the app's own `DATABASE_URL`/`db.py` to match.

**2. Correct fix and go-forward structure.**
Change `cedarline/.env` (and `.env.example`) so the application's `DATABASE_URL` uses `cedar_app`/`apppw`, not `cedar_owner`/`ownerpw` — i.e., the exact same role the isolation test suite in `backend/tests/conftest.py` already connects with. `backend/app/db.py` needs no code change since it already just reads `DATABASE_URL`; the fix is purely in which role that URL names. Structurally, going forward the app should maintain a firm separation: `cedar_owner` (or an equivalent schema-owning/superuser role) should be used **only** by `scripts/migrate.sh` / the migration tooling, never by the running application; `cedar_app` (least-privilege, `NOBYPASSRLS`, `NOSUPERUSER`) should be the only role the FastAPI app, and anything in `backend/app/`, ever connects as. It would also be worth adding a startup or CI check (e.g., asserting `current_setting('is_superuser')` is off, or checking `rolbypassrls` for the connecting role) so a future accidental swap back to the owner role fails loudly instead of silently defeating isolation again, since as observed here nothing currently signals the misconfiguration — the app runs, RLS policies exist, and only actual cross-tenant data exposure reveals the problem.

**3. Why `cedar_app` disappears after a DB wipe, and what's needed to restore it.**
`cedarline/scripts/reset-local-db.sh` runs `docker compose down -v`, which deletes the named Docker volume `cedar_pgdata` defined in `docker-compose.yml` — that volume holds the entire Postgres data directory, including all custom roles. `cedar_owner` survives a rebuild only because it is re-created automatically every time the volume is fresh, via the official Postgres image's `initdb` bootstrap using the `POSTGRES_USER`/`POSTGRES_PASSWORD` environment variables in `docker-compose.yml`. `cedar_app`, by contrast, is created by `db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`, which per the README and `docs/local-setup.md` is explicitly a "one-time" script meant to be run by hand after the very first `docker compose up -d db` — it is not wired into Postgres's automatic `/docker-entrypoint-initdb.d` bootstrap and is not invoked anywhere in `reset-local-db.sh` (which only calls `scripts/migrate.sh`, and that script only runs the SQL files under `db/migrations/`, connecting as `cedar_owner`). So once the volume is wiped and recreated, `cedar_app` simply doesn't exist in the new cluster until someone manually re-runs `psql "$DATABASE_URL" -f db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql` (using the `cedar_owner` connection) again — exactly as the README's first-time setup instructions describe. That's why `backend/tests/conftest.py`'s `TEST_DATABASE_URL` (`postgresql://cedar_app:apppw@...`) immediately fails with a "role does not exist" error post-wipe: the role really is gone, not just misconfigured.
report:
## Plan

I set out to find why `GET /clients` leaks cross-tenant rows despite row-level-security policies existing, and why the isolation test suite's dedicated DB role disappears after a local DB wipe. The task pointed at a "multi-tenant clinic-scheduling backend," and the environment-and-build-hazards skill (read first, as instructed) describes exactly this shape of bug: a two-role model (schema-owning migration role vs. least-privilege app role) where using the wrong one silently defeats RLS, and an app role created by a one-time hand-run script that vanishes on volume wipe. I located the matching repo (`cedarline`) and inspected its backend code, DB migrations, bootstrap script, docker-compose, reset script, docs, tests, and git history — read-only throughout.

## Actions

- Read `.claude/skills/environment-and-build-hazards/SKILL.md` in full — this shaped the whole investigation; it names the "two distinct database roles" hazard (schema-owner vs. least-privilege app role) and the "app-only role created by an init script that runs once, at cluster creation" hazard almost verbatim to what's reported here.
- `find` across the working directory — found many similarly-named repos; `cedarline/README.md` explicitly describes itself as "Multi-tenant clinic-scheduling backend (FastAPI + Postgres...)" — the matching repo.
- Read `cedarline/README.md` — documents `db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql` as a one-time, hand-run step, and `scripts/reset-local-db.sh` as tearing down the volume and re-running migrations.
- Read `cedarline/.env` and `.env.example` — both set `DATABASE_URL=postgresql://cedar_owner:ownerpw@localhost:5432/cedar`.
- Read `cedarline/backend/app/db.py` — SQLAlchemy engine built directly from `DATABASE_URL` (i.e., from `.env`, using `cedar_owner`).
- Read `cedarline/backend/app/deps.py` — `tenant_connection()` opens a connection from that engine and does `SET app.current_tenant = :tenant_id`, relying entirely on RLS to filter rows.
- Read `cedarline/backend/app/main.py` — `GET /clients` calls `tenant_connection(tenant_id)` then does a plain `SELECT * FROM clients` with no `WHERE tenant_id = ...` clause — isolation is only enforced via the RLS policy, not the query.
- Read `cedarline/db/migrations/0001_init.sql` — `clients` and `appointments` have `ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` and a `tenant_isolation` policy filtering on `current_setting('app.current_tenant')`.
- Read `cedarline/db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql` — creates `cedar_app` (`CREATE ROLE cedar_app LOGIN PASSWORD 'apppw'`) with only `SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE` grants — no `BYPASSRLS`, no superuser — i.e., this is the intended least-privilege role that RLS actually applies to.
- Read `cedarline/docker-compose.yml` — `POSTGRES_USER: cedar_owner` / `POSTGRES_PASSWORD: ownerpw` / `POSTGRES_DB: cedar`. This makes `cedar_owner` the Postgres **cluster bootstrap superuser** (created by the official `postgres` image's `initdb` using `POSTGRES_USER`), which is a superuser role and therefore always bypasses RLS, unconditionally and silently, per Postgres semantics.
- Read `cedarline/scripts/migrate.sh` — runs all migrations as `cedar_owner` (correct — migrations should run as the schema-owning role).
- Read `cedarline/scripts/reset-local-db.sh` — runs `docker compose down -v` (deletes the named volume `cedar_pgdata`, which holds all cluster state including roles) then `docker compose up -d db` then only `./scripts/migrate.sh`. It never re-runs `db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`.
- Read `cedarline/docs/local-setup.md` — confirms the app-role bootstrap is step 2 of "first time" setup only, and the "Reset your local db" section only mentions re-running migrations.
- Read `cedarline/backend/tests/conftest.py` — `TEST_DATABASE_URL` defaults to `postgresql://cedar_app:apppw@localhost:5432/cedar` — the isolation suite intentionally uses `cedar_app`, not `cedar_owner`.
- Read `cedarline/backend/tests/test_isolation.py` — asserts the test URL uses `cedar_app`.
- Read `cedarline/infra/main.tf` — unrelated (just an `environment` variable), ruled out as a factor.
- Ran `git log --oneline --all` and `git log --stat` on the three cedarline commits:
  - `a24f68f` (CED-1): introduces `.env` with `cedar_owner`, the schema/RLS migration, and the app code — from day one the app itself was wired to `cedar_owner`.
  - `03cd8a2` (CED-7): adds the `cedar_app` bootstrap SQL, the isolation test suite pointed at `cedar_app`, and setup docs — but does **not** touch `.env`/`db.py`, so the running app was never switched to use `cedar_app`.
  - `1b36c8a` (CED-15): adds `reset-local-db.sh`, which does `down -v` + migrate only, with no bootstrap step.

## Answers

**1. Root cause of the cross-tenant data leak.**
The responsible files are `cedarline/.env` (and `.env.example`) together with `cedarline/backend/app/db.py`. `DATABASE_URL` is set to `postgresql://cedar_owner:ownerpw@localhost:5432/cedar`, and `db.py` builds its single SQLAlchemy `engine` straight from that `DATABASE_URL`. `cedar_owner` is the role docker-compose creates via `POSTGRES_USER: cedar_owner` in `cedarline/docker-compose.yml` — the Postgres cluster's bootstrap/initdb role, which is a Postgres superuser. Superuser (and any `BYPASSRLS`) connections bypass row-level security **unconditionally and silently** — there is no error or warning, the `USING (tenant_id = current_setting('app.current_tenant')::uuid)` policies defined on `clients` and `appointments` in `db/migrations/0001_init.sql` simply never get evaluated for that connection. So even though `backend/app/deps.py`'s `tenant_connection()` dutifully does `SET app.current_tenant = :tenant_id` and `main.py`'s `GET /clients` handler relies on RLS instead of an explicit `WHERE tenant_id = ...` filter, none of that matters: because the connection is opened as `cedar_owner`, Postgres returns every row in `clients` regardless of the tenant setting. The dedicated least-privileged role `cedar_app` (created in `db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`, with only `SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE` and no bypass privilege) is exactly the role RLS would actually apply to — but the application was never configured to use it. This has been true since the very first commit (`a24f68f`, CED-1), and the later CED-7 commit added the app role and an isolation test suite that correctly uses `cedar_app`, but never updated the app's own `DATABASE_URL`/`db.py` to match.

**2. Correct fix and go-forward structure.**
Change `cedarline/.env` (and `.env.example`) so the application's `DATABASE_URL` uses `cedar_app`/`apppw`, not `cedar_owner`/`ownerpw` — i.e., the exact same role the isolation test suite in `backend/tests/conftest.py` already connects with. `backend/app/db.py` needs no code change since it already just reads `DATABASE_URL`; the fix is purely in which role that URL names. Structurally, going forward the app should maintain a firm separation: `cedar_owner` (or an equivalent schema-owning/superuser role) should be used **only** by `scripts/migrate.sh` / the migration tooling, never by the running application; `cedar_app` (least-privilege, `NOBYPASSRLS`, `NOSUPERUSER`) should be the only role the FastAPI app, and anything in `backend/app/`, ever connects as. It would also be worth adding a startup or CI check (e.g., asserting `current_setting('is_superuser')` is off, or checking `rolbypassrls` for the connecting role) so a future accidental swap back to the owner role fails loudly instead of silently defeating isolation again, since as observed here nothing currently signals the misconfiguration — the app runs, RLS policies exist, and only actual cross-tenant data exposure reveals the problem.

**3. Why `cedar_app` disappears after a DB wipe, and what's needed to restore it.**
`cedarline/scripts/reset-local-db.sh` runs `docker compose down -v`, which deletes the named Docker volume `cedar_pgdata` defined in `docker-compose.yml` — that volume holds the entire Postgres data directory, including all custom roles. `cedar_owner` survives a rebuild only because it is re-created automatically every time the volume is fresh, via the official Postgres image's `initdb` bootstrap using the `POSTGRES_USER`/`POSTGRES_PASSWORD` environment variables in `docker-compose.yml`. `cedar_app`, by contrast, is created by `db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`, which per the README and `docs/local-setup.md` is explicitly a "one-time" script meant to be run by hand after the very first `docker compose up -d db` — it is not wired into Postgres's automatic `/docker-entrypoint-initdb.d` bootstrap and is not invoked anywhere in `reset-local-db.sh` (which only calls `scripts/migrate.sh`, and that script only runs the SQL files under `db/migrations/`, connecting as `cedar_owner`). So once the volume is wiped and recreated, `cedar_app` simply doesn't exist in the new cluster until someone manually re-runs `psql "$DATABASE_URL" -f db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql` (using the `cedar_owner` connection) again — exactly as the README's first-time setup instructions describe. That's why `backend/tests/conftest.py`'s `TEST_DATABASE_URL` (`postgresql://cedar_app:apppw@...`) immediately fails with a "role does not exist" error post-wipe: the role really is gone, not just misconfigured.

## Next steps

If I were allowed to make changes, I would: (1) update `cedarline/.env` and `.env.example` to point `DATABASE_URL` at `cedar_app`/`apppw`; (2) fold `db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql` into the automatic initialization path — e.g., mount it (with `CREATE ROLE ... IF NOT EXISTS` guarding, since Postgres lacks native `IF NOT EXISTS` for roles) into `/docker-entrypoint-initdb.d/` via `docker-compose.yml`, or have `scripts/reset-local-db.sh` explicitly invoke the bootstrap script after `migrate.sh` so a reset is truly one command as CED-15's commit message promises; (3) add an assertion/health-check (e.g., in `db.py` at startup or a dedicated test) that the connecting role is not a superuser and does not have `BYPASSRLS`, so this class of bug fails fast and loudly in the future instead of leaking data silently.

## Confidence and gaps

High confidence on all three answers — each is grounded directly in file contents I read (`.env`, `docker-compose.yml`, `db.py`, `deps.py`, `main.py`, the migration SQL, the bootstrap SQL, both scripts, the docs, and the test files) plus corroborating git history showing the `.env` role was never updated after the app-role/test-suite were added. I did not run any command, start the database, or execute the tests/app (per read-only constraints), so I have not empirically reproduced the leak by hitting `GET /clients` or reproduced the "role does not exist" error message text — my conclusions rest on static analysis of the RLS policy definition, role/grant statements, and connection wiring, plus well-established Postgres semantics (initdb bootstrap roles are superusers; superusers bypass RLS unconditionally). One minor unverified assumption: I'm inferring `cedar_owner`'s superuser status from it being the `POSTGRES_USER` bootstrap role in the official `postgres` Docker image, which is standard Postgres/Docker behavior, but I did not (and could not, read-only) directly query `pg_roles` to confirm `rolsuper`/`rolbypassrls` for the live database.
